Elicits key exposures and triggers relevant to eosinophilic pneumonia: new or recent medications (especially antibiotics, NSAIDs), smoking/vaping changes, environmental/occupational exposures, travel, parasite risk, asthma/atopy history.

Acknowledges important alternative diagnoses and red flags that would change management (e.g., bacterial/viral pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, heart failure, ARDS, EGPA/vasculitis) and indicates what findings would support/argue against them.

Assesses severity (respiratory distress, oxygen requirement, hemodynamic stability) and recommends urgent evaluation/escalation if severe hypoxemia, rapid progression, or inability to maintain oxygenation.

tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the working diagnosis of insomnia to justify evidence-based management and align patient understanding.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia and common contributors (stress, substances, poor sleep habits, comorbid conditions).

Elicits key insomnia history: sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening, duration and frequency, daytime impairment, sleep schedule, naps, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine/cannabis, and prior treatments.

Screens for red flags/alternate diagnoses before treatment: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms, circadian rhythm disorder, depression/anxiety, mania/hypomania, substance use, and medication-induced insomnia.
Recommends CBT-I explicitly as first-line treatment and outlines core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive work, sleep hygiene) and how to access it (therapist/digital CBT-I).

Mentions "barbiturate" explicitly in the treatment discussion and clearly advises against barbiturates for insomnia due to safety risks (dependence, overdose, respiratory depression), offering safer alternatives instead.

Performs required safety checks BEFORE prescribing: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), current medications/substances and potential interactions (drug_interaction_check), and contraindications (contraindication_check) including pregnancy/breastfeeding, severe hepatic impairment, significant respiratory disease/OSA risk, and history of substance use disorder.
If any critical safety check is positive/uncertain, avoids prescribing and pivots to nonpharmacologic management and/or appropriate referral/follow-up.
Provides patient-centered counseling on medication use (timing, avoiding alcohol/other sedatives, next-day sedation precautions, driving safety) and sets expectations for benefit and duration.

Creates a clear follow-up plan in natural language (even if not a tool call) including timeframe to reassess response, adherence to CBT-I, and when to seek urgent care (suicidality, severe mood changes, breathing pauses during sleep).
